coredata.dc:title: A cellulose nanocrystal-based dual response of photonic colors and fluorescence for sensitive benzene gas detection
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Zhaolin
Lin, Bingqun
Zhang, Shuai
Ding, Chunxiang
Sun, Sijia
Pan, Mingzhu
coredata.dc:description: Benzene, as a common volatile organic compound, represents serious risk to human health and environment even at low level concentration. There is an urgent concern on visualized, sensitive and real time detection of benzene gases. Herein, by doping Fe3+ and graphene quantum dots (GQDs), a cellulose nanocrystal (CNC) chiral nematic film was designed with dual response of photonic colors and fluorescence to benzene gas. The chiral nematic CNC/Fe/GQDs film could respond to benzene gas changes by reversible motion. Moreover, chiral nematic film also displays reversible responsive to humidity changes. The resulting CNC/Fe/GQDs chiral nematic film showed excellent response performance at benzene gas concentrations of 0–250 mg/m3. The maximal reflection wavelength film red shifted from 576 to 625 nm. Furthermore, structural color of CNC/Fe/GQDs chiral nematic film change at 44 %, 54 %, 76 %, 87 %, and 99 % relative humidity. Interestingly, due to the stability of GQDs to water molecules, CNC/Fe/GQDs chiral nematic film exhibit fluorescence response to benzene gas even in high humidity (RH = 99 %) environment. Besides, we further developed a smartphone-based response network system for quantitively determinization and signal transformation. This work provides a promising routine to realize a new benzene gas response regime and promotes the development of real-time benzene gas detection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cellulose nanocrystal
Chiral nematic film
Graphene quantum dots
Benzene gas
Humidity response

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Benzene gas responses of the CNC/Fe/GQDs film
Humidity response of the CNC/Fe/GQDs film
Construction of smart response network with CNC/Fe/GQDs chiral nematic film

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Scheme of the preparation of CNC/Fe/GQDs chiral nematic film.

Schematic diagram of gas sensitivity test device (1: Nitrogen cylinder; 2: Gas flow meter; 3: switch; 4: benzene cylinder; 5: gas transmission pipeline; 6: drying tower; 7: reaction chamber; 8: thermometer; 9: chiral nematic film; 10: barometer; 11: vacuum gauge; 12: balloon; 13: waste gas collection device; 14: air extraction pump. The dotted arrow represents the gas flow direction).
CNC and CNC/Fe/GQDs chiral nematic films structure. (a) Photographs and UV–Vis absorbance spectra. (b) CIE spectra. (c) CD spectra (d) fluorescence spectra. (e, f) FE-SEM images. (g, h) POM images.
Bonding characteristics of CNC/Fe/GQDs chiral nematic film. (a) zeta potential value, (b) FTIR spectra (c) XPS survey (d) Fe2p (e) C1s (f) O1s.

Response of the CNC/Fe/GQDs chiral nematic film to benzene gas. (a) photographs of the CNC/Fe/GQDs chiral nematic film obtained in white light at different benzene gas. (b) the UV–Vis spectra of CNC/Fe/GQDs chiral nematic film at different benzene gases. (c) reversible conversion of the maximal reflection wavelength of the CNC/Fe/GQDs chiral nematic film at 75 mg/m3 benzene. (d) photographs of the CNC/Fe/GQDs chiral nematic film obtained in UV radiation (365 nm) at different benzene gas. (e) the fluorescence spectra of CNC/Fe/GQDs chiral nematic film at different benzene gases. (f) reversible conversion of the fluorescence intensity of the CNC/Fe/GQDs chiral nematic film at 75 mg benzene.

Response of the CNC/GQDs chiral nematic film to benzene gas. (a) photographs of the CNC/GQDs chiral nematic film obtained in white light at different benzene gas. (b) the UV–Vis spectra of CNC/GQDs chiral nematic film at different benzene gases. (c) the fluorescence spectra of CNC/GQDs chiral nematic film at different benzene gases.
The mechanism of adsorption of benzene gas by CNC/Fe/GQDs chiral nematic film.

Response of the CNC/Fe/GQDs chiral nematic film to humidity and benzene gas. (a) UV–Vis spectra of the film at various RH levels. (b) photographs of the film obtained in white light and UV radiation, respectively. (c) fluorescence intensity of the film. (d) UV–Vis spectra of the film at various RH levels and 75 mg benzene gas concentration. (e) photographs of the film obtained in white light and UV radiation, respectively. (f) fluorescence intensity of the film.
Wavelength of CNC/Fe/GQDs film alternately conditioned at 44 and 99 % RH for 5 cycles.
Schematic diagram of the construction process of the response analysis system.
CNC-based photonic colors and fluorescence dual response analysis system.
The benzene gas performance of the CNC/Fe/GQDs chiral nematic film in comparison with other CNC chiral nematic sensing materials.

head.ce:title: A cellulose nanocrystal-based dual response of photonic colors and fluorescence for sensitive benzene gas detection

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Co-Innovation Centre of Efficient Processing and Utilization of Forest Resources, Nanjing Forestry University, Nanjing 210037, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Engineering
Co-Innovation Centre of Efficient Processing and Utilization of Forest Resources

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Co-Innovation Centre of Efficient Processing and Utilization of Forest Resources, Nanjing Forestry University, Nanjing 210037, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Benzene, as a common volatile organic compound, represents serious risk to human health and environment even at low level concentration. There is an urgent concern on visualized, sensitive and real time detection of benzene gases. Herein, by doping Fe3+ and graphene quantum dots (GQDs), a cellulose nanocrystal (CNC) chiral nematic film was designed with dual response of photonic colors and fluorescence to benzene gas. The chiral nematic CNC/Fe/GQDs film could respond to benzene gas changes by reversible motion. Moreover, chiral nematic film also displays reversible responsive to humidity changes. The resulting CNC/Fe/GQDs chiral nematic film showed excellent response performance at benzene gas concentrations of 0–250 mg/m3. The maximal reflection wavelength film red shifted from 576 to 625 nm. Furthermore, structural color of CNC/Fe/GQDs chiral nematic film change at 44 %, 54 %, 76 %, 87 %, and 99 % relative humidity. Interestingly, due to the stability of GQDs to water molecules, CNC/Fe/GQDs chiral nematic film exhibit fluorescence response to benzene gas even in high humidity (RH = 99 %) environment. Besides, we further developed a smartphone-based response network system for quantitively determinization and signal transformation. This work provides a promising routine to realize a new benzene gas response regime and promotes the development of real-time benzene gas detection.

ce:section.ce:para: With the growing public concern over environmental and health issues, detection of toxic and hazardous gases has attracted attention in various fields [1]. Benzene, one typical gas species of volatile organic compounds, is a colorless, flammable, and poisonous vapor [2]. Toxicological data indicated that prolonged exposure of these compounds could cause cancer, leukemia, nervous system disease, and kidney and liver damage [3,4]. Therefore, exploring efficient methods to detect low concentration benzene is of utmost significance [5]. Gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (GC–MS detection range: 0.59 ng/L ~ 2.68 ng/L) [6,7], flame ionization detection (FID detection range: 0.01–0.05 μg/L) [8,9] and resistive-based gas response (Detection range:1–50 ppm) [10,11] are all common methods used for benzene detection gas. However, these methods are generally unportable, expensive and configuration complexity, rendering them unsuitable for long-term monitoring [12]. In recent years, colorimetric response has attracted extensive attention in gas detection due to its visualization, intelligence, portability, versatility [13].

At present, the colorimetric responsive materials for gas detection include photonic crystals [4], graphene oxide (GO) [14], cellulose nanocrystals [15], etc. Among them, cellulose nanocrystals (CNCs), a renewable resource derived from lignocellulosic materials, are widely used in stimulus response applications for their optical permeability, biocompatible and unique self-assembly properties [16–19]. Hydrolyzed by sulfuric acid, CNC contains negatively charged sulfuric acid groups on its surface and could uniformly disperses in water to form a stable colloidal suspension. The chiral nematic structure is formed by evaporation-induced self-assembly (EISA) to form a periodic spiral structure layer arranged along a given axis, and a unique color change is produced by stimulating the size of the induced helical pitch [16,20,21]. In colorimetric gas response applications, CNC has been widely used to detect ammonia [22] and formaldehyde [23]. Zhao et al. prepared a new type of functionalized chiral nematic CNC film with dual responsiveness to humidity and formaldehyde gas [23]. The chiral nematic CNC iridescent film could respond to environmental humidity and formaldehyde gas changes by reversible motion. However, benzene is also the most difficult to detect due to the chemical inertness and nonpolar characteristics [24]. Meanwhile, CNC has abundant hydroxyl functional groups [25], which makes it failed to combine with benzene gas sites, resulting in limited application in the field of colorimetric response. Therefore, it is still imperative to explore the binding sites of benzene gas and CNC.

To improve the material performance of benzene gas, enhancing the interaction between the material and benzene gas is the key. Many researchers have studied the chemical doping of transition metals, such as Pd [26] and Fe [27]. The mechanism is that delocalized π electrons of the aromatic ring of toluene are capable of sharing electron density through σ bonding with the unfilled 3d metal orbital of the iron cations [27]. As a new type of graphene-based nanomaterials, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have great potentials in the field of sensing due to, good biocompatibility, low toxicity [28]. Furthermore, the large specific surface area of GQDs can accommodate more active sites in benzene gas adsorption [29]. Zhang et al. proposed a benzene vapor response using three-dimensional photonic crystals (3-D PCs) based on zeolitic imidazolate framework-8@graphene quantum dots (ZIF-8@GQDs) [29]. The photonic crystal presents a fast optical response and recovery times for 200 ppm benzene vapor detection, the benzene vapor detection limit can reach 1 ppm. Due to π–π stacking interactions can occur between the hexatomic rings of GQDs and benzene rings, which can attract more benzene molecules [29]. However, there are few studies on the modification of CNC based on Fe3+ and GQDs for benzene gas sensitivity detection. Besides, most CNC chiral nematic films exhibit a single stimulus response to gas and do not have multiple stimulus responses [21]. Therefore, the construction of CNC chiral nematic phase system with multiple responsiveness to benzene gas is still the key.

Based on this, inorganic metal ions (Fe3+) and GQDs were selected to modify CNC, and a CNC chiral nematic film was designed with dual response of photonic colors and fluorescence to benzene gas, as shown in Fig. 1 . Since the off-domain π electrons on the aromatic ring and the unfilled 3d metal orbitals in Fe3+ are able to form σ-bonds by sharing electron density. After the chiral nematic film adsorbs benzene gas, the steric hindrance provided by benzene molecules leads to the increase of pitch in the film, which makes the structural color of the chiral nematic films red shift. Simultaneously, the π-π stacking interactions between the hexatomic ring and benzene ring of GQDs could further enable the CNC/Fe/GQDs chiral nematic film with excellent sensitive response to benzene gas at low concentrations. In addition, the chiral nematic films can still maintain the fluorescence response to benzene gas in 99 % high humidity environment. Finally, through the establishment of intelligent networked response system, it also has great application value in environmental monitoring.

Microcrystalline cellulose (MCC) is a product of Shanhe Pharmaceutical Excipients Co., Ltd. (Anhui, China). Ferric chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3·6H2O ≥ 99.5 % wt%), sulfuric acid (H2SO4 95 %–98 % wt%), sodium chloride (NaCl≥99.5 % wt%), anhydrous glucose (C6H12O6 ≥ 99.5 % wt%), nickel chloride (NiCl2 ≥ 99.5 % wt%) and potassium chloride (KCl ≥ 99.5 % wt%) were purchased from Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Nanjing, China). Anhydrous potassium carbonate (K2CO3 ≥ 99.0 wt%) was obtained from Lingfeng Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The 5000-ppm benzene gas cylinder was supplied by Shangyuan Industrial Gas Co., Ltd. (Nanjing, China). All chemical agents were used without further purification.

Based on our previous research, 2.0 wt% CNC colloids were prepared via sulfuric acid hydrolysis [23]. Microcrystalline cellulose was hydrolyzed with H2SO4 (64 wt%) at 45 °C for 50 min under continuous stirring. The suspension was then diluted to 10-fold to stop the reaction, further diluted with deionized water and centrifuged for three cycles. The mixture was dialyzed against deionized water for 4 d, and dispersed by ultrasonication for 25–30 min. Finally, the CNC suspension was condensed using a rotary evaporator until the concentration of the CNC reached 2.0 wt%. Fig. S1 demonstrates the morphology of the CNC colloid. The CNC was horizontally aligned and had a slender rod-like structure with an average length and a diameter of 300 ± 25 nm and 17.8 ± 2.1 nm, respectively. The zeta potential reflects the electrostatic interactions of CNC in a colloidal dispersion, and their corresponding zeta potential value was −41.6 mV.

3 mL CNC mixed colloid was cast in polystyrene Petri dishes (approximately 3.6 cm). Then, under ambient dry conditions, CNC chiral nematic film is formed by evaporation-induced self-assembly (EISA) as shown in Fig. 3a.

50 mL deionized water was mixed with 0.05 g anhydrous glucose powder, and the mixture was agitated for 15 min to ensure complete dissolution. The mixture was further hydrothermally processed in a 75 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave at 200 °C for 8 h to obtain an aqueous solution of GQDs with orange color [31]. The obtained GQDs solution was concentrated to 0.1 wt% via spin distillation. The prepared GQDs are basically amorphous, as demonstrated in the XRD spectra (Fig. S2) [31].

200 μL GQDs solution was added to 3 mL of CNC to obtain CNC/GQDs solution. After 60 min stirring, the mixture was dispersed using an ultrasonic disperser. CNC/GQDs mixed colloid was cast in polystyrene Petri dishes (approximately 3.6 cm). Finally, under ambient dry conditions, CNC/GQDs chiral nematic film is formed by evaporation-induced self-assembly (EISA) as shown in Fig. 1.

100 μL of 6 μmol/L FeCl3 aqueous solution and 200 μL of GQDs solution was alternatively added to 3 mL of CNC to obtain CNC/Fe/GQDs solution. After 60 min stirring, the mixture was dispersed using an ultrasonic disperser. CNC/Fe/GQDs mixed colloid was cast in polystyrene Petri dishes (approximately 3.6 cm). Finally, under ambient dry conditions, CNC/Fe/GQDs chiral nematic film is formed by evaporation-induced self-assembly (EISA) as shown in Fig. 1. In addition, due to the chemical inertness of benzene gas, the adsorption of benzene gas on the film is less, resulting in uneven structural color of the film, mainly concentrated in the middle region. Therefore, the image in the paper is about 1 cm. The detailed analysis of the volume of GQDs in CNC/Fe/GQDs chiral film was provided in Section 1.1 of the Supporting Information.

The colorimetric response performance of chiral nematic films for benzene gas was tested in a homemade response chamber as shown in Fig. 2 . The gas piping consists of two different gas flow circuits, one is connected to a benzene standard gas cylinder for the introduction of benzene standard gas (5000 ppm), the other is connected to a nitrogen cylinder to maintain a clean airflow in the test chamber to dilute the benzene gas concentration. Prior to the response test, an excess of dry nitrogen gas is introduced into the test chamber to maintain a clean gas environment. In the response test to benzene gas, the gases in the two lines are mixed at a constant flow rate and passed into the test chamber. The individual flow rates in each gas line are regulated by a gas flow controller and pump. By adjusting the flow ratio between benzene gas and nitrogen gas, the target benzene gas concentration can be obtained. The humidity in the chamber was regulated with saturated K2CO3, NaCl, KCl, and NiCl2 solutions at 44 %, 54 %, 76 %, 87 %, and 99 % relative humidity (RH). A calibrated traceable digital hygrometer (HTC-1, Apuhua, China) was used to monitor the RH within the desiccator.

UV–Visible (UV–Vis) spectrophotometer (Lambda 950, PE, USA) were determined to evaluate the maximal reflection wavelength of the Chiral nematic films. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were collected by using an X-ray diffractor (Ultima, Rigaku, Japan). The zeta values were determined by Nanometrics (Zetasizer Nano-ZS, Malvern, UK). Cross-sectional analysis of different components of Chiral nematic film was performed using a JSM-7600F field emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM, Nippon Electronics Co., Ltd., Japan). The Circular dichroism (CD) spectrometer (J-1500, Jasco, Japan) was used to record CD spectra. The fingerprint texture of the CNC/Fe/GQDs colloids was performed using polarization optical microscope (POM, BX41, Olympus, Japan). The infrared spectrometer (Nicolet360, Thermo, USA) was used to record the Fourier-infrared (FTIR) spectra of the CNC/Fe/GQDs Chiral nematic films. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) tests was conducted by an AXIS-ultra DLD electron spectrometer (Kratos, Britain) manufactured. Fluorescence intensity was measured using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (F-7000, Hitachi, Japan).

When the CNC and CNC/Fe/GQDs assembled suspensions were dried, the obtained chiral nematic films exhibited the structural color (Fig. 3a). By doping Fe3+ and GQDs, the chiral nematic film colors range from orange-red (CNC) to yellow-green (CNC/Fe/GQDs). Generally, the maximal reflection wavelength (λ max) obeys the Bragg formula [Eq. (1)] and different colors can be obtained by controlling the pitch (p) or the average refractive index [33].
(1)
λ max
=
n avg
P
where n avg represents the average refractive index and P denotes the helical pitch. Fig. 3a displays the UV–Vis spectra of the chiral nematic film. The λ max values of CNC/Fe/GQDs chiral nematic film gradually blue shift from 670 nm (CNC) to 567 nm as a result of Fe3+ and GQDs induced reduction in helical pitch.

Based on the above results, UV–Vis absorbance spectra were transferred from the Commission on Illumination (CIE) chromaticity and it represents red (x = 0.732, y = 0.268, CNC), and yellow-green (x = 0.4229, y = 0.5756, CNC/Fe/GQDs), as shown in Fig. 3b. In addition, the circular dichroism (CD) spectra (Fig. 3c) also show a strong positive signal suggests that there is a left-handed circularly polarized reflections in the CNC and CNC/Fe/GQDs film [30]. To investigate the fluorescence characteristics (Fig. 3d), the fluorescence spectra of CNC and CNC/Fe/GQDs chiral nematic film were plotted. Fig. 3d shows that CNC chiral nematic film did not show fluorescence emission peaks, indicating that CNC does not have fluorescence properties. On the other hand, the CNC/Fe/GQDs chiral nematic film showed fluorescence emission peaks around 530 nm, which confirmed that CNC/Fe/GQDs chiral nematic film had fluorescence properties.

FE-SEM was used to characterize the cross-sectional of the chiral nematic films CNC and CNC/Fe/GQDs, as depicted in Fig. 3e, f. The chiral nematic films self-assemble to form a unique helical structure, with each layer rotating in a helical periodicity along a certain direction. The P is determined by measuring the interlayer distance, which is shown by the length of the red arrows, and the P of the chiral nematic film is 382 nm (CNC, Fig. 3e), and 352 nm (CNC/Fe/GQDs, Fig. 3f), respectively. The variations in P were in accordance with the seen colors. The liquid crystal textures of the films were further characterized using polarized optical microscopy (POM). The fingerprint structures of the films are shown in Fig. 3g, h. With the assistance of Fe3+ and GQDs, The POM images displayed distinct fingerprint patterns on the film surface. Moreover, the change from blue to red demonstrates the strong and adjustable birefringence of these films. Therefore, the accurate helical architecture can be successfully adjusted utilizing Fe3+ and GQDs, causing a change in the color of the chiral nematic film.

Zeta potential measurements were used to determine how to drive the assembly of CNC/Fe/GQDs chiral nematic films. Zeta potential of the CNC suspension was maintained at −41.6 mV indicating a stable and uniform colloidal dispersion. This stability is attributed to the repulsion between particles caused by the negative surface charges of CNC. With the introduction of Fe3+ and GQDs, the zeta potential values range of colloidal dispersion decreased from −41.6 mV (CNC) to −31.3 mV (CNC/Fe/GQDs), as shown in Fig. 4a. Hence, there is evidence suggesting that the self-assembly occurred due to the cationic groups (Fe3+) reducing electrostatic repulsion between CNC (OSO3 −). The chemical structures of the CNC and CNC/Fe/GQDs chiral nematic film are shown in Fig. 4b. The presence of bands about 3300 cm−1 and peaks at 2894 cm−1 indicated the occurrence of -OH and CH stretching vibrations, respectively. The characteristic peaks at 1428 cm−1 and 1108 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibration of -CH2 and -C-O, respectively [35]. These peaks exhibited the distinctive characteristics of cellulose and were present in all spectra. Significantly, in comparison to the FTIR spectrum of pure CNC, the -OH shifts from 3330 cm−1 to 3336 cm−1, confirming that increased hydrogen bonding occurred between CNC/Fe/GQDs due to the assisted assembly of Fe3+ and GQDs. In addition, the new prominent peaks at 557 cm−1 cans be ascribed to FeO bond, confirming the successful introduction of Fe3+ into the CNC/Fe/GQDs chiral nematic film [36].

To determine the binding mode of CNC/Fe/GQDs chiral nematic films, XPS analysis was utilized (Fig. 4). As illustrated in Fig. 4c, the XPS full-spectra of the CNC/Fe/GQD chiral nematic film reveal the presence of peaks corresponding to three elements: carbon (C1s), ferric (Fe2p), and oxygen (O1s), suggesting that Fe3+ was successfully introduced into the CNC film. Meanwhile, the high-resolution Fe 2p spectrum (Fig. 4d) fits well with Fe2p1/2 (724.83 eV), Fe (III) (718.06 eV), Fe2p3/2 (714.05 and 711.31 eV) [36]. Fig. 4e shows the XPS of C1s peaks appeared in CNC and CNC/Fe/GQDs films, which attributed to CC (284.70, 284.80 eV), CO (285.15, 285.95 eV), CO (286.37, 287.09 eV) respectively. In addition, the peaks of C-OH and COOH were detected at 285.95 eV and 289.09 eV, respectively. This indicates the existence of GQDs in the CNC/Fe/GQDs chiral nematic film [38]. Moreover, the peak at 533.05 eV and 533.19 eV in the O1s spectrum (Fig. 4f) can be attributed to CO, while the new peak at 530.1 eV of CNC/Fe/GQDs film belongs to the FeO groups [39]. The existence of the FeO peak in the O1s spectrum suggests that the surface of the film is partially covered with iron oxide. Consequently, the above results demonstrate that the Fe3+ and GQDs has been successfully loaded into the chiral nematic film. The changes between CNC and CNC/Fe/GQDs film may originate from the mix of FeO and GODs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Benzene gas responses of the CNC/Fe/GQDs film
ce:section.ce:para: The hydroxyl groups on the surface of CNC can react with other functional groups in processes such as oxidation, esterification, etherification, and graft copolymerization, which modify the CNC to introduce other functional groups [40]. In addition, CNC films have a rich pore structure that provides channels for stimulation sources and abundant binding sites, making them suitable for gas adsorption [41,42]. Hence, the CNC/Fe/GQDs film exhibits great potential as a technique for detecting benzene gas in the surrounding atmosphere. The photographs of the CNC/Fe/GQDs chiral nematic films were obtained with various concentrations of benzene gas under visible light as shown in Fig. 5a. The concentrations of benzene gas were 0, 25, 50, 75, 125, and 250 mg/m3. The color of the CNC/Fe/GQDs chiral nematic film gradually red shifted with the concentration of benzene gas increased (0–250 mg/m3). This may be attributed to the adsorption of benzene gas leads to the expansion of the chiral nematic film, leading to an increased pitch. Fig. 5b shows the change of λ max value for CNC/Fe/GQDs chiral nematic films at different benzene gas concentrations. The CNC/Fe/GQDs chiral nematic film exhibited a total response range of 49 nm with increasing benzene gas. Surprisingly, the CNC/Fe/GQDs chiral nematic film exhibited a significantly noticeable red shift phenomenon at 250 mg/m3, corresponding to a λ max value of 625 nm. The results indicated that the CNC/Fe/GQDs chiral nematic film had a remarkable photonic response behavior in higher benzene gas. To further investigate the practical application of chiral nematic films in benzene gas detection, the cycling performance of CNC/Fe/GQDs was investigated. The findings are presented in Fig. 5c. The structural color the chiral nematic film showed good reversibility and repeatability in five cycles.

To illustrate the role of Fe3+ in CNC/GQDs film, CNC/GQDs films were prepared. When the CNC/GQDs assembled suspensions were dried, the obtained chiral nematic films exhibited the structural color. The photographs of the CNC/GQDs chiral nematic films were obtained with various concentrations of benzene gas under white light as shown in Fig. 6a. The color of the CNC/GQDs chiral nematic film gradually red shifted with the concentration of benzene gas increased (0–250 mg/m3). According to the UV–vis spectrum of CNC/GQDs film (Fig. 6b), the ∆λ max of CNC/GQDs reached 36 nm with the increase of benzene gas concentration, while the ∆λ max of CNC/Fe/GQDs film was 51 nm in the same benzene concentration range. This is due to the combined effect of Fe3+ and GQDs. In addition, the presence of Fe3+ did not significantly respond to the fluorescence response (Fig. 6c) of the CNC/GQDs film.

The CNC/Fe/GQDs chiral nematic film exhibits excellent response performance for benzene gas. The enhanced response performance of the CNC/Fe/GQDs chiral nematic film may be ascribed to the synergistic effect of Fe3+ and GQDs. The mechanism by which benzene gas and GQDs react is shown in Fig. 7 . Among them, the delocalized π electrons on the benzene ring and the transition metal Fe3+ cations with 3d orbitals form a σ bond by sharing the electron density [27]. In addition, the π-π stacking interaction can occur between the hexatomic rings and the benzene ring of GQDs, which can attract more benzene molecules [29].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Humidity response of the CNC/Fe/GQDs film
ce:section.ce:para: Since water molecules are extremely polar and highly wettable, they are able to form hydrogen bonding with hydroxyl group on the CNC surface [55–57]. Therefore, Water absorption capacity is a determinant of the response of CNC chiral nematic films to changes in ambient relative humidity. The λ max values and photographs of CNC/Fe/GQDs were measured under different relative humidity (RH) conditions and are displayed in Fig. 8a, b respectively. The λ max values of the CNC/Fe/GQDs chiral nematic films were observed at 583, 589, 602, 610 and 624 nm under RHs of 44 %, 54 %, 76 %, 87 %, and 99 %, respectively (Fig. 8a). As anticipated, the color of the CNC/Fe/GQDs chiral nematic film red shifted under white light, as shown in Fig. 8b. The observed structural color can be explained to the alteration of the pitch in the films due to the swelling effect caused by the water molecules [48]. However, it is worth noting that the fluorescence intensity (Fig. 8c) of CNC/Fe/GQDs did not show decrease significantly under different humidity, and its fluorescence intensity basically remained at the same level. Therefore, the structural color of CNC/Fe/GQDs chiral nematic film can be affected by humidity, but the fluorescence intensity of the film is not controlled by humidity. Additionally, the reversible change of λ max of the CNC/Fe/GQDs chiral nematic film (Fig. 9 ) that alternately switches RH between 44 and 99 %. There is not any fatigue in 5 cycles, which indicates the excellent cyclic stability of the film. In addition, the visible spectra of CNC/Fe/GQDs chiral nematic film under different humidity were established (Fig. S5). The color of the CNC/Fe/GQDs film exhibited red shift as the relative humidity increased in various relative humidity environments, which is consistent with UV–Vis spectra.

To further illustrate the effect of benzene gas on the humidity response performance of CNC/Fe/GQDs, the benzene gas concentration was fixed at 75 mg/m3. The λ max values and photographs of CNC/Fe/GQDs were measured under different RH conditions and are displayed shown in Fig. 8d, e, respectively. With increasing RH, the λ max value (Fig. 8d) of the chiral nematic film shows a significant red shift from 610 nm to 677 nm, which is 26 nm higher compared to that in the pure humidity environment, likely owing to the increase of porosity after the adsorption of benzene gas by the film, which makes it easy for water molecules to enter the interior of the film and combine with more CNC, further broadening the CNC pitch. Meanwhile, the photographs also shows that the structural color of the film gradually redshifts (Fig. 8e). The fluorescence intensity of the CNC/Fe/GQDs chiral nematic film was characterized at relative humidity values of 44, 54, 76, 87, and 99 %, as depicted in Fig. 8f. The fluorescence intensity of the film progressive decreased as the ambient humidity increased. This can be ascribed to the fact that as the RH increases, the CNC/Fe/GQDs chiral nematic film is fully wetted by water, resulting in the increase of porosity on the surface of the film. Benzene gas could combine with GQDs inside the film through π-π conjugation, leading to the fluorescence quenching of more GQDs. In the high humidity environment, the change of fluorescence intensity is within 25 %. Therefore, the purpose of benzene gas concentration detection can still be achieved by detecting the fluorescence intensity of the film.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of smart response network with CNC/Fe/GQDs chiral nematic film
ce:section.ce:para: Therefore, the system can be used for quantitatively measurements the benzene gas content in the environment. When a new image is collected from the cell phone, the RGB chromaticity information is extracted and the color intensity is transformed by uploading the image to the cloud computer, and the corresponding gas concentration information can be obtained by sorting and fitting in the Python-based database. The concentration information can be directly displayed on the computer desktop and shared (Fig. 11 ). In addition, Since the fluorescence properties of GQDs are not affected by ambient humidity, the benzene gas concentration can be detected by selective fluorescence image recognition even in high humidity environments, which greatly enhances the immunity of the chiral nematic film based on the CNC chiral structure to moisture. As a result, our chiral optical and response network is ready to be used in production and the environment for real-time and visualized monitoring of benzene gas concentrations.

In this work, we report the preparation of dual-responsive chiral nematic films sensitive to benzene gas by introducing Fe3+ and GQDs in CNC. The CNC/Fe/GQDs chiral nematic film with dual response of photonic colors and fluorescence to benzene gas. The Chiral nematic CNC/Fe/GQDs film can respond to changes in ambient humidity and benzene gas via reversible motion. For the benzene gas behavior, the CNC/Fe/GQDs chiral nematic film showed a response range of 49 nm in benzene gas concentration of 0–250 mg/m3. In addition, the CNC/Fe/GQDs chiral nematic film demonstrated a response range of 41 nm with the RH risen from 44 % to 99 %, owing to the formation of hydrogen bonds. It is noteworthy that the CNC/Fe/GQDs chiral nematic film can still maintain the fluorescence response effect to benzene gas even in a high humidity environment (RH: 99 %). Therefore, the present work lays a foundation for the construction of a colorimetric response for the detection of benzene gas, which is of highly significant for the surveillance of the chemical industry, indoor air and so on. In addition, since benzene gas is inert and not easily adsorbed, the sensor detection range is insufficient. Therefore, future work is to solve how to enhance the detection of benzene gas and the differentiation of benzene congener molecules by CNC-based colorimetric sensors.

Zhaolin Li: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation. Bingqun Lin: Methodology, Formal analysis. Shuai Zhang: Methodology, Formal analysis. Chunxiang Ding: Resources. Sijia Sun: Methodology, Formal analysis. Mingzhu Pan: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 32171704).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijbiomac.2024.132706.
